# Master 99% of Claude Cowork in 52 Minutes (Full Course)

**Channel**: Brock Mesarich | AI for Non Techies
**Duration**: 52:44
**Language**: English (auto-generated)
**URL**: https://youtube.com/watch?v=9BEWaO_eEFE
**Transcribed**: 2026-03-23 03:58

---

## Organized Notes

**What Is Claude Co-work**

- Claude Co-work is the **desktop app** from Anthropic — free, runs on Mac and Windows
- Unlike basic chat, it can **read files, connect to apps, learn workflows, and run tasks while you sleep**
- Most people use Claude at **10% capacity** — treating it like a search engine instead of an AI employee
- Four superpowers that differentiate it: **Claude MD file, Skills, Connectors, Scheduled Tasks**

**Step Zero: Project Folders**

- Must **point Claude to a folder** on your computer before doing anything
- Without a folder: no memory, no context, no skills, no file access — just a chatbot
- With a folder: **remembers who you are**, loads custom skills, reads files, gets smarter each session
- Think of each folder as a **different phone** — work phone vs personal phone with different apps and identity
- Folder structure: **Claude MD file, skills, documents, outputs** — keep it organized like an office space

**Superpower 1: Claude MD File (Identity)**

- A plain text file Claude reads **before every interaction** — your permanent identity and instructions
- Without it: Claude starts fresh every conversation with no context
- Six things to include: **who you are, audience, tone, business context, rules, what to avoid**
- Think of it as an **onboarding packet** that Claude reads every day and never forgets
- A **context builder skill** can auto-generate this by asking you seven questions

**Superpower 2: Skills (Custom Commands)**

- Skills are like **apps for your AI** — install once, trigger with a keyword
- Each skill is a **markdown file** with detailed instructions Claude follows every time
- Skills auto-load based on conversation context — no need for verbose prompting
- Can create skills by: **uploading zip files, using "Create with Claude," or converting perfected workflows**
- Skills live in the **customize > skills** section of Co-work's sidebar

**Seven Daily Skills Demonstrated**

1. **Morning Briefing** — scrapes calendar, email, AI news; generates an Apple Swiss-styled HTML dashboard
2. **Research Assistant** — produces structured research documents with executive summary, sources, and data
3. **Meeting Notes to Action Items** — paste transcript, get clean summary with specific action items and deadlines
4. **Slide Deck Generator** — creates full visual presentations from a single sentence with smooth animations
5. **Visual Explainer** — builds interactive web pages to visualize complex topics with diagrams (credit: **Nico Balon**)
6. **Diagram Generator** — creates professional **Excalidraw-compatible JSON diagrams** from descriptions
7. **Skill Creator** — meta-skill that helps build new custom skills through guided conversation

**Superpower 3: Connectors (App Integrations)**

- Without connectors: must manually copy-paste between Claude and other apps
- With connectors: Claude can **read calendar, access Gmail, access Google Drive, post to Slack** directly
- **38+ native integrations** available inside Claude (Canva, GitHub, Gmail, Google Calendar, Slack, etc.)
- **Claude in Chrome** connector gives Claude ability to use your browser and fill out forms
- For apps without native integration, use **Zapier MCP** — connects to **8,000+ apps** with 30,000+ actions
- Setup: create MCP server on zapier.com/mcp, copy URL, paste into Co-work's connector settings
- Can set tools to **"always allow"** to skip manual approval for each action

**Superpower 4: Scheduled Tasks (Automation)**

- New feature — set a time and Claude runs tasks **automatically with no input**
- Requires laptop to be open for the task to trigger
- Example schedules: **morning brief at 8 AM, end-of-day wrap-up at 6 PM**
- Setup: describe the task, set frequency (hourly, daily, weekdays, weekly), and Claude handles the rest
- **End-of-day wrap-up** breaks down what Claude accomplished, unfinished items, and tomorrow's priorities
- Connectors + scheduled tasks + skills = **AI employee running your business on autopilot**

**Claude Co-work vs Claude Code**

- Same AI (Claude), **two completely different tools**
- **Co-work**: desktop assistant, visual app, click-and-type interface, sandboxed, built for everyone
- **Claude Code**: coding agent, lives in terminal/IDE, reads codebases, writes and runs code, ships to production
- Co-work is **Claude Code without the complexity** — no terminal knowledge needed
- Co-work is the **perfect middle ground for non-technical users**
- If comfortable with **IDEs like Cursor or VS Code**, try Claude Code instead

**The Full System Stack**

- Claude MD (identity) + Skills (workflows) + Connectors (app access) + Scheduled Tasks (automation) = **complete AI employee**
- Set up once, works forever — train once and it follows the system every time

## What This Means for Your Work

**The folder-as-identity concept maps directly to your 9-platform architecture.** Each of your platforms -- e-Bangsamoro, e-Negosyo, MoroMarket, Tarbiyyah-MS, BangsamoroHR, SBP, OBCMS, Parliamentarian, IPP -- should have its own Claude Co-work folder with a tailored Claude.md, platform-specific skills, and relevant reference documents. You already do this in Claude Code with project-level CLAUDE.md files, but Co-work extends this to your non-coding workflows: policy drafting for OOBC, legislative research for the BTA Parliament, and training content for the SEED Initiative's 124+ courses.

**The seven daily skills demonstrated here have direct equivalents in your existing 129-skill library, but the Co-work versions serve a different audience.** Your Claude Code skills are developer-facing. Co-work skills could serve your non-technical collaborators at the Office of the Chief Minister, BTA Parliament staff, or MoroTech team members who do not use the terminal. The Morning Briefing skill that scrapes calendar and email into an HTML dashboard could be adapted for BARMM officials -- a daily legislative briefing that pulls from your Vault's bangsamoro-laws index and shows upcoming Parliament sessions, pending bills, and committee assignments.

**The connectors + scheduled tasks combination creates the "AI employee" pattern you need for MoroTech operations.** As a solo founder running a social enterprise plus 9 platforms plus OOBC consulting, you cannot manually monitor everything. Scheduled tasks could automate: daily checks on e-Bangsamoro deployment status, weekly summaries of cooperative registrations in e-Negosyo, morning briefings that compile BARMM governance news. Connect Gmail, Google Calendar, and Slack through Co-work's 38+ native integrations so Claude handles the operational overhead while you focus on development and policy work.

**The Claude Co-work vs Claude Code distinction clarifies which tool to use for which role.** For your developer work (React 19, Django, PostgreSQL, shipping code), Claude Code in the terminal remains your primary tool. For your OOBC Consultant and legislative research work (drafting policy memos, reviewing BOL provisions, preparing briefing documents), Claude Co-work's visual interface with file access and connectors may be more efficient. You do not need to choose one -- run Co-work for policy work and Claude Code for platform development simultaneously.

**The Skill Creator meta-skill is worth adopting for rapidly building Co-work skills for your non-technical stakeholders.** If BTA Parliament staff or OOBC colleagues need Claude-powered workflows but cannot write skills themselves, you can use the Skill Creator to build them custom skills through guided conversation -- a bill summary skill, a committee schedule skill, a training module generator for SEED Initiative. This extends your impact beyond what you personally code.

**The Zapier MCP connector for 8,000+ apps fills integration gaps your platforms will eventually need.** For e-Negosyo cooperative marketplace, you might need to connect to Philippine payment gateways, government registration APIs, or SMS notification services. For BangsamoroHR, integration with existing BARMM payroll or attendance systems. Zapier MCP through Co-work gives you a rapid prototyping path for these integrations before you build dedicated API connections in Django.

### How This Can Improve Your Claude Skills and Workflows

**The four-superpower framework -- identity, skills, connectors, scheduled tasks -- mirrors the architecture you already use in Claude Code, but Co-work surfaces opportunities to extend it to non-technical collaborators.** Your 129-skill library is developer-facing and terminal-bound. Co-work's skill format is simpler markdown that non-coders can trigger through a visual interface. This means skills like /legislative-briefer, /policy-recommendation, and /speech-writer could be ported into Co-work versions for BTA Parliament staff or OOBC colleagues who need AI-assisted drafting without touching the command line.

**The Skill Creator meta-skill demonstrated in the video is a less structured version of your /expert-builder workflow, and comparing the two reveals where your approach is stronger.** Brock's Skill Creator uses guided conversation to generate a skill file. Your /expert-builder goes further -- it builds skills with frontmatter, progressive disclosure, evaluation criteria, and reference file integration. But Co-work's approach has a distribution advantage: you could use /expert-builder to create the skill architecture, then export a simplified version to Co-work for stakeholders who only need the execution layer, not the development layer.

**The scheduled tasks pattern maps directly to what /sp-dispatching-parallel-agents and /sp-subagent-driven-development do in your Claude Code environment, but with a time-trigger instead of a task-trigger.** Your subagent patterns dispatch work based on complexity and domain. Co-work's scheduled tasks dispatch work based on time. Combining both -- using Claude Code agents for development sprints and Co-work scheduled tasks for operational monitoring (deployment health checks, daily legislative digest generation, vault index updates) -- creates a two-layer automation system where each tool handles the work it is best suited for.

**The connector ecosystem fills a gap in your current /research-pipeline and /notebooklm workflows.** Right now, feeding sources into NotebookLM or running research pipelines requires manual source gathering. Co-work connectors to Gmail, Google Drive, and Google Calendar could automate the first stage of /research-pipeline -- collecting raw inputs from your communication channels and calendar -- before passing structured data to Claude Code for deeper analysis through /auto-research or /context.

**The folder-as-identity pattern validates your project-level CLAUDE.md approach and suggests extending it to your /session-summary and /connect skills.** Each Co-work folder is essentially a persistent context window -- the same function your /context skill serves in Claude Code. The difference is Co-work loads context automatically on every interaction, while /context requires manual invocation. Consider redesigning /connect and /session-summary to write outputs that both Claude Code and Co-work can consume, so switching between the two tools does not break your continuity of thought.
